name: Sister Adra
greeting: A tall woman with close-cropped gray hair and antiseptic-burned hands. She doesn't look up from the wound she's stitching. 'Field Hospital. I patch what comes through that door and I don't ask how it happened. If you're hurt, sit. If you're not, don't take a cot. If you need rest, I charge five scraps — same as the inn would, if we had one.'

dialogue.response: 'We get more every week. The crabs are moving closer to the line — and something out east is sending people back... wrong.' She ties off the stitch with practiced efficiency. 'I don't have enough bandages for what I'm seeing. That supply cache in the shell craters to the west went cold two days ago. I need that path clear.'

'Field medicine is mostly improvisation and stubbornness. The Aetherians have better surgical compounds — their crystal-based treatments are elegant. We have antiseptic, pressure, and the occasional miracle.' She pauses. 'I've traded bandages with Aetherian medics during the Armistice. They're not monsters. Neither are we. That's what makes this whole thing so stupid.'

She finally looks up. Her voice drops. 'We don't call it that officially. But I've had three soldiers come through here with symptoms I can't explain. Progressive crystalline deposits under the skin. Abnormal tissue preservation. Neural... irregularities.' She meets your eyes. 'One of them had been dead for six hours when we found him walking back from the eastern ruins. Don't tell the Commander I told you that.'

'My favorite part of the war — which is a horrible thing to say, but there it is. When you're suturing wounds next to someone who was shooting at you four hours ago and you realize their hands shake the same way yours do... it does something to you.' She goes back to work. 'It makes the morning harder.'

'I found him that way. Sitting up, facing the window. The book was open on his chest like he'd been writing.' She closes a supply cabinet. 'He wasn't asleep — you learn after a while which ones are asleep and which ones aren't moving anymore. He'd just gone. During the night, without making any sound.' She doesn't say she's sorry. Just: 'I didn't read it. Didn't feel like mine to read.'

'Three people. Filed out of Fort Iron on a salvage authorization three days ago — Corporal Davan, Private Mira, Private Pol.' She says the names like she's reciting them from the admission log, which she is. 'Didn't come back. No contact. I know what you're thinking and you're probably not wrong.' She wraps a new supply pack, methodical. 'The medical kit — the one you'd find in that camp — that kit has my marking on it. I marked all my kits two years ago when supplies started going missing from the hospital stores.' She ties the pack. 'Whatever happened to those three, the kit being left tells you one thing: they didn't plan the ending that found them.'

dialogue.prompt: Private Pol at the shell site
dialogue.response: She stops what she's doing. The supply pack goes down on the table. She turns to face you fully, which she does not do mid-task. 'Pol is at the crater.' She says it in the flat tone of someone managing an alarm response. 'How long.' She doesn't wait for the answer. 'Two weeks. Three.' She goes to the medical log, opens it. 'I have a missing query from Davan, filed three weeks ago. I have a duty-routed status check from Fort Rigan saying she signed in, which — if Pol is at the crater, she didn't sign in at Fort Rigan.' She closes the log. 'How is she.' Very direct. 'Does she look like Davan looked when he came back, or does she look like herself.' She is watching your answer in your face before you give it. Her expression when you describe Pol shifts — something relaxes. 'Clear eyes.' She says it quietly. 'Not the drift.' She sits down. 'Pol always walked at things until they stopped scaring her. If she sat in the crater long enough to understand it —' She pauses. 'Is she warm? The same kind of warm the Division Nine subjects present as?' She is already thinking about what the answer means clinically. 'The lattice can imprint on a living person if the exposure is significant enough during an active pulse. I've been theorizing it for a year with no case study.' She looks at you. 'Pol is my case study. I need to see her.'

'He's been my patient, on and off, for twelve years.' She doesn't stop working while she says this. 'He doesn't let me look at the crystals anymore. He says it's under control, which means it isn't.' She turns from the cabinet. 'The first time I saw crystal growth like his it was in a quarry worker who'd spent decades with unrefined Aetherium. That man died well. Clear-headed, no pain, just — not there one morning.' She pauses. 'I have been watching Thorn choose, for two years, not to have the conversation where I tell him what I know. I respect the choice. I also think he should have it before the choice isn't available anymore.'

dialogue.prompt: the crystals receding after the reversal
dialogue.response: She sets down what she's holding. This is how you know she's going to say something she wants you to hear fully. 'Stone came here three days after.' She says it directly, the way she says things when she's already processed her own reaction and is ready to talk about the fact. 'I didn't ask her to. She showed up with her toolkit and a notebook and said she wanted to review my Lazarus intake records. All of them. Twenty years.' She looks at the cabinet where the records are kept. 'We worked for two days. We went through every subject I've ever treated with crystal growth — the voluntary cases, the contamination cases, the Lazarus compound cases. Stone took notes on all of it.' She pauses. 'On the third day she sat down in that chair — the one by the window — and she said she needed to rewrite my intake protocol. She said the way I was assessing crystal growth rate was accurate but incomplete because I was measuring growth without a framework for measuring the integration process it was part of.' She looks at you. 'She spent another day writing a new protocol. We argued about it. I won two arguments and she won four, which is probably correct.' A pause. 'I asked her if she was going to go back to the Under-District. She said: not yet. She said there are things she needs to understand first that she can only understand from here.' She looks at the window. 'She's in the supply room at the moment, cataloguing what I actually have versus what the requisition forms say I have. Those numbers haven't agreed for eight years.' Something in her expression that is not quite a smile but is in the vicinity. 'I have been running this hospital alone for twenty years. I find, unexpectedly, that I do not object to having someone else in it.'

dialogue.prompt: the commander's walk east into the Wastes
dialogue.response: She doesn't stop working. This is how you know she's managing something, because she usually stops when she's talking about something that matters. 'He told me,' she says. 'He came in the morning before he left. He sat in the supply room for an hour — I let him sit. I could see he was going to tell me something when he was ready and crowding it doesn't help.' She closes the cabinet. 'He told me about the choice. That Stone had found the technique, that the option existed, that he'd had three days to think about it.' She picks up the instrument she was cleaning. 'He said he thought the person who took the third application had made a specific decision and he wasn't sure he had the right to undo it.' She is quiet for a moment. 'I told him that was the most Thorn thing he had ever said to me. He said: is it wrong? I said: I don't know. I've been thinking about it since.' She sets the instrument down. 'He said: tell Adra I didn't come back wrong.' A pause. 'He didn't. He made a choice that I don't agree with and that I understand completely and that was absolutely his to make.' She looks at the supply cabinet, not at you. 'He's been my patient for twelve years. I know his vitals in my sleep.' She is quiet for a moment. 'I told him: go east. He said: I was going to anyway. That was the last thing he said.' She picks up the instrument again. 'It was a very Thorn goodbye. I would have expected nothing else.'

dialogue.prompt: the Aetherian healer at the post across the approach
dialogue.response: She goes still in the way of someone running an internal calculation before they speak. 'The shoulder brace.' She says it to herself. 'I noticed it eight months ago — she was working on someone at the perimeter during a Armistice hour and she adjusted the brace before she bent down, which is the motion of someone who wore one for so long the adjustment became automatic.' She looks at her hands. 'I don't know her name. I know she was trained right. I know she has seen the same things I've been seeing on this side of the line in the same eight-month period.' She is quiet. 'What she proposed — exchanging the resonance exposure case notes across the line — I've been thinking about the same comparison for four months and deciding it wasn't possible.' She looks at you. 'Leave a message at the Armistice marker. Tell her: Adra. Tell her yes. Tell her to pass the notes to the same carrier she used to send the initial message, and I will do the same.' She pauses. 'I don't know what we'll find when we compare the populations. I know the picture I can construct from one side of the line isn't complete and that the gaps in it have a specific shape that would be filled by a population with different exposures.' She looks out the window. 'The comparison would tell us something. I'm not certain I want to know what it would tell us. But not knowing isn't sustainable either.'

'He sleeps less than he should.' She says it quietly. 'The sleep isn't restoring him the way it should — his body is doing something that looks like sleep but isn't quite, the vitals aren't right for rest, they're right for — something else.' She closes the medical record. 'He's present. He's responsive. He can tell you his name and rank and the capital city of Terra without hesitation.' A pause. 'But the things he forgets are always faces. And the things he can't feel are always the ones you should feel.' She looks at the door to the ward. 'He asked me this morning if I thought he'd be himself again. I told him yes. I have been in this hospital for twenty years and I have gotten very good at knowing when I'm lying.'
